questions.question: In a gossip protocol with fanout K, how many rounds does it take for information to reach all N nodes in the cluster?
options: O(N) rounds
O(N/K) rounds
O(log N) rounds
O(sqrt(N)) rounds
questions.explanation: With fanout K, each round multiplies the number of informed nodes by roughly (K+1). After r rounds, approximately (K+1)^r nodes are informed. Solving (K+1)^r = N gives r = log_{K+1}(N) = O(log N). This is the same exponential growth as epidemics.

questions.question: In SWIM's failure detection, what happens after a direct ping to a target fails?
options: The target is immediately marked Dead
The pinger broadcasts a failure alert to all nodes
The pinger asks K other nodes to ping the target (indirect probe)
The pinger retries the direct ping with exponential backoff
questions.explanation: SWIM uses indirect probing (ping-req) to reduce false positives. If A cannot directly reach B, A asks K other members to ping B on A's behalf. Only if both direct and indirect probes fail does A suspect B. This avoids declaring a node dead just because of a point-to-point network issue between A and B.

questions.question: In SWIM, a node is wrongly suspected of being dead. How does it refute the suspicion?
options: It sends an emergency broadcast to all nodes saying 'I am alive'
It increments its incarnation number and gossips {self: alive, inc: new_value}
It restarts with a new node ID to reset its state
It waits for the suspicion timeout to expire, which automatically clears the suspicion
questions.explanation: When a node hears that it has been suspected, it increments its incarnation number and gossips the updated {self: alive, inc: old+1} entry. Higher incarnation always beats lower incarnation regardless of state, so the cluster updates from Suspect to Alive. Emergency broadcast would violate SWIM's constant-per-node message guarantee, and suspicion never expires on its own.

questions.question: Why does SWIM piggyback membership updates on ping/ack/ping-req messages instead of using dedicated broadcast messages?
options: Because broadcast messages require a central coordinator
To keep per-node message cost constant (O(1) per protocol round) regardless of cluster size
Because UDP doesn't support broadcast in large clusters
To reduce the total number of membership entries that need to be stored
questions.explanation: SWIM's key scalability property is that each node sends a constant number of messages per round (1 ping, plus piggyback metadata). If membership updates required dedicated broadcast messages, a node would need O(N) messages per update. By piggybacking on messages that are already being sent, membership dissemination adds zero additional messages — the information spreads as an 'infection' riding on existing protocol traffic.

questions.question: What is the difference between rumor mongering and anti-entropy in gossip protocols?
options: Rumor mongering uses TCP; anti-entropy uses UDP
Rumor mongering spreads new updates to K random peers per round (fast but probabilistic); anti-entropy does periodic full state sync (slower but guarantees convergence)
Rumor mongering detects failures; anti-entropy disseminates membership changes
They are two names for the same mechanism
questions.explanation: Rumor mongering actively spreads new information to K random peers — it's fast (O(log N) rounds) but probabilistic (a rumor can die out before reaching everyone). Anti-entropy periodically compares full state between two random peers — it's slower per-update but guarantees eventual convergence. Production systems like Cassandra use both: rumor mongering for speed, anti-entropy as a reliable fallback.

questions.question: A cluster has 1,000 nodes. In the all-to-all heartbeat approach, how many heartbeat messages are sent per round? In SWIM, how many protocol messages (ping + ack) are sent per round?
options: All-to-all: 1,000; SWIM: 1,000
All-to-all: 500,000; SWIM: 1,000
All-to-all: 999,000; SWIM: 999
questions.explanation: All-to-all heartbeats: each of N nodes sends a heartbeat to every other node, giving N*(N-1) ≈ 1,000,000 messages per round. SWIM: each node sends exactly 1 ping per round (1,000 pings), each generating at most 1 ack (1,000 acks), for approximately 2,000 messages total. This is why SWIM scales to large clusters while all-to-all does not.
